title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered to restore historic buildings. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Julian, The Medieval Castle Project
'I’ve always been a history buff, but touching stones laid eight hundred years ago was something else,' Julian says. [51]The biggest challenge was the physical strain of moving heavy materials without modern machinery, which I found exhausting.[/51] [48]I was pleasantly surprised to find that my chronic shoulder tension disappeared after a few weeks of manual labor.[/48] [53]I realized that our ancestors had a level of patience and craftsmanship that we’ve almost entirely lost in the digital age.[/53] [50]I found that my skills as an accountant were unexpectedly useful for managing the project's limited budget for materials.[/50] [47]Initially, the isolation of the site was quite unsettling, but I eventually grew to love the peace.[/47] It was a deeply grounding experience.
texts.B:
### Clara, The Victorian Greenhouse Restoration
Clara helped restore a botanical glasshouse. 'It was like working in a giant, fragile puzzle,' she recalls. [49]The experience taught me that conservation is as much about the future as it is about the past.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the level of technical detail required; I spent three days just cleaning a single ornate window frame.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how quickly I adapted to the physical demands of climbing ladders all day.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the opportunity to work alongside master craftsmen who shared their secrets so freely.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen the 'call for volunteers' on a local news segment, I’d have missed this life-changing opportunity.[/56] [50]I’ve noticed that the attention to detail I developed has made me much more thorough in my studies.
texts.C:
### Sam, The Remote Highland Bothy
Sam spent a month repairing mountain shelters. 'It was basic living—no electricity, no running water,' he says. [55]It took a while to adjust to the lack of social contact, which made the first few evenings quite difficult.[/55] [51]We struggled with the unpredictable weather, which often halted our progress for days at a time.[/51] [53]I found that the project gave me a sense of purpose that my corporate job never provided.[/53] [48]I noticed that my general fitness levels reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect I hadn't planned for.[/48] [49]The trip taught me to value the essentials in life and discard the rest.[/49] [52]I’d initially worried that I wouldn't be useful because I had no construction experience, but I learned quickly on the job.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Mediterranean Village Project
Elena helped restore abandoned stone houses. 'The heat was the main character in our story,' she laughs. [47]I hadn't expected the intense midday sun to dictate our entire schedule, which was a huge adjustment.[/47] [54]The highlight for me was the communal dinners with the villagers, who treated us like returning heroes.[/54] [55]I found the slow pace of the work incredibly frustrating at first, as I wanted to see immediate results.[/55] [50]I realized that my fluency in the local language was a key factor in building trust with the community.[/50] [56]I wouldn't have known about the project if my university hadn't offered it as an elective module.[/56] [52]I had been concerned that the work would be too repetitive, but every stone had its own unique history.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by their adaptation to a new environment or physical routine?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) was startled by how quickly she adapted to ladders, Julian (A) adjusted to isolation, and Elena (D) to the sun-dictated schedule.

questions.question: notes a positive physical change that occurred as a result of the work?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) lost his shoulder tension, and Sam (C) reached an all-time high in fitness.

questions.question: expresses a new perspective on the importance of the work they were doing?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) saw conservation as being about the future, and Sam (C) learned to value the essentials.

questions.question: notes that a personal skill or background was beneficial to the project?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used his accounting skills, and Elena (D) used her language fluency.

questions.question: mentions a specific factor that made the physical work particularly difficult?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions moving heavy stones without machinery, and Sam (C) mentions the weather.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns that turned out to be unfounded?
questions.explanation: Sam (C) worried about his lack of experience, and Elena (D) worried the work would be too repetitive.

questions.question: reflects on a deeper realization about human nature or personal satisfaction?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) reflects on the patience of ancestors, and Sam (C) found a sense of purpose missing in his job.

questions.question: mentions the value of social interactions during the project?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) valued working with master craftsmen, and Elena (D) loved communal dinners with villagers.

questions.question: describes a period of psychological or mental adjustment at the start?
questions.explanation: Sam (C) struggled with the lack of social contact, and Elena (D) found the slow pace frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges how they first became aware of the volunteering opportunity?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) saw it on the news, and Elena (D) found it through her university elective.
